articles.title: Agora-1: The Multi-Agent World Model
articles.summary: Agora-1 is a multi-agent world model that enables up to four players to interact within the same generated world in real-time. Players are matched into a shared deathmatch simulation, where every participant interacts with the same generated world simultaneously. Everything you experience is generated by Agora-1 in real time, with the model simulating player interactions from their actions and maintaining a shared world state across participants. In effect, Agora-1 functions as a learned game engine.

Misconceptions about the UNIX Philosophy
articles.summary: Misconceptions about the UNIX Philosophy include the idea that it has been inherited by Windows and other operating systems, that Unix philosophy involves multiple tiny programs communicating over pipes, and that this communication must be plain text. Jonathan Blow's comments highlight a deep misunderstanding of the UNIX philosophy, suggesting that it is more complex than it appears. The article also touches on the concept of microservices and their relationship to the UNIX philosophy, as well as the potential for microservices to be seen as a criticism of Unix philosophy in modern systems.

articles.title: Haiku OS runs on M1 Macs now
articles.summary: Haiku OS is now running on Macs powered by the M1 chip, offering a new level of customization and performance for developers. The Haiku ARM port uses an unmodified version of the M1 chip, allowing it to boot from USB drives like any PC. This makes it ideal for users looking to upgrade their Mac hardware without needing to replace the entire system. While there are still some limitations, such as a broken USB connection, this is a significant improvement over traditional UEFI-based systems. The Haiku ARM port aims to provide developers with a more seamless and efficient way to work on their projects across different platforms.

articles.title: Elon Musk has lost his lawsuit against Sam Altman and OpenAI
articles.summary: Elon Musk has lost his lawsuit against Sam Altman and OpenAI after nine California jurors returned a unanimous verdict that his lawsuits had been filed too late. While the trial focused on whether and when Altman and the other defendants had made and broken promises to Musk, his case failed to convince jurors that he had a valid claim. In particular, OpenAI had advanced a statute of limitations defense , which sought to prove that any harms Musk sought to litigate had taken place before 2021. The end of the case means that one major threat to OpenAI — a possible restructuring — is now off the table ahead of its reported IPO. Microsoft, which Musk sued for aiding and abetting OpenAI's alleged breach of charitable trust, welcomed the verdict.

articles.title: A Linux-like kernel in a browser tab - deep dive in the BrowserPod architecture
articles.summary:
A Linux-like kernel in a browser tab - deep dive in the BrowserPod architecture

X Facebook Y Combinator LinkedIn Reddit Email WhatsApp

A few months ago, we released BrowserPod: an in-browser sandbox powered by WebAssembly that runs completely client-side. Under the hood, it provides a WebAssembly-native kernel designed from scratch to run in the browser while being compatible with many applications originally designed for Linux. This kernel is the critical feature that makes BrowserPod powerful enough to run real-world, full-stack apps completely in the browser.

The core functions of modern operating systems are often misunderstood by developers accustomed to more abstracted forms of coding. A kernel is a fundamental component of any operating system that provides access to hardware and manages the execution of applications. In BrowserPod, the WebAssembly kernel acts as an abstraction layer between the user interface and the underlying hardware, allowing for efficient rendering and processing of applications running in the browser.

articles.title: Don't answer the first question
articles.summary: In my work on Perfetto, a performance debugging tool, one question I get often is: "how do I split a Perfetto trace into multiple files?" Instead of answering directly, I say: "there isn’t an easy way to do that, but what’s leading you to collect traces large enough to want to split?” This is one of my golden rules at work. When a user asks me something “weird”: don't answer the first version of the question. On the surface this might appear like I'm talking about the XY problem, but that stops one step short. It treats the user's stated question as a puzzle to decode: figure out what they really meant, answer that, move on. I think we can go much further. Instead, the confusion that produced the wrong question is itself an opening, and the conversation it sparks is valuable to both sides. The user walks away with a better mental model of the tool.
